free_text: Department of Physics,Polytechnic of South
Bank,London,England.
Gamma emission cross sections for 23 elements using
3.29 MeV neutron inelastic scattering
Stabilized 400 kV Van de Graaff accelerat
operated at 300 kV and 100 microAmp current.
The neutrons were produced by the D(d,n)3He
reaction.
.The incident energy of 3.29 MeV was determined from
a voltage calibration and a tabulation of neutron
energies from charged particle reactions.
.The samples of the natural elements were in powder
form (purities better than 99%) in polythene
containers (3.5cm height x 4cm diameter), weighting
between 17.8 and 132.6 g.
The gamma radiation of the excited target
nuclei was detected and the corresponding signals
fed to a multi-channel analyzer.
A NaI crystal (7.6x7.6cm2), at 90 deg to the
neutron beam served as the gamma detector, coupled
to a Canberra 1413 AMP and a ND 2400 multichannel
analyzer.
.The photopeak efficiency was determined by using
reference gamma sources from Amersham.
.The fast flux was measured using the
reaction 115In(n,n ,gamma)115(m)In with a metastable
state at 335 keV.
.The thermal component (0.09% of the fast) was
determined by the reaction 115In(n,gamma)116(m)In
with a metastable state at 417 keV.
.The beam stability was monitored by Harwell 2000
series beam monitoring equipment and a Li-glass
detector.
.The energy-resolution of the detection system was
7.7% for the 662 keV line of 137Cs.
.Photopeak area integration based on the
convolution technique (see REL-REF).
- convolution
technique
The well-known 847 keV line of the
reaction Fe(n,n ,gamma)Fe(m) with a differential
cross section between 70.6 and 68.0 mb/sr at 90.deg.
Mean of four different measurements was used.
.The heading ANG refers to the common scattering
angle.
.Corrections were made:
* for prompt radiation from the experimental
environment  due to scattered neutrons
from target and sample,
* for delayed radiation due to activation,
* for Bremsstrahlung background and gamma
self-absorption in the samples.
The total errors vary between 10.6 and 14.6%.
The experimental uncertainty was composed from the
statistical errors and following contributions:
The uncertainty in the diff. cross section
of the line 847 keV in Fe
The uncertainty in the efficiency calibration
due to the source,
The uncertainty in the geometrical uncertainty
during calibration plus the statistical errors.
The uncertainty in the  self-absorption
correction.
The uncertainty in the correction for
attenuation and multiple scattering.
The uncertainty in the incident flux monitoring
The uncertainty in the time-dependent
background correction.
The uncertainty due to dead-time variations in
the analyzer.
The uncertainty due to possible errors in
weighting and positioning the samples.
